title: [rootless] Naive container -> host networking
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind feature

**Description**

I've been trying to do some naive inter-pod networking without having to do the legwork of probing and passing the host IP into my containers. Unfortunately it seems like something is now preventing me from (ab)using slirp gateway/DNS IPs to connect to services listening on the host or in other containers (exposed via published ports in their pod). I think this may have worked in the past prior to podman starting to use the slirp `--disable-host-loopback` option.

My overarching goal is to provide a simple default host to connect to which will make inter-pod networking Just Work (TM) in development environments where it's not practical for me to rely on more robust DNS and load balancing that would happen on something like k8s (maybe noteworthy, I'm using `podman play kube` to bring pods up). This use case sounds like it would be adequately solved by a solution for #6863, but that seems like it'll be a non-trivial change, and for my purposes treating this as a container -> host problem would also be fine and likely simpler to solve/reject.

I'd like to either be able to specify something like the magic slirp gateway IP `10.0.2.2` or some magic DNS which resolves to all non-loopback addresses on the host side so that the container side could attempt to perform client side DNS failover and eventually connect to a service published by the host on a non-loopback address. From a few brief netcat pings between containers, it seems like just DNS answering with all IPs when questioned about a magic hostname would work and not allow any surprising connectivity on my Fedora 32 machine.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. `(host)$ nc -lvvk 12345

2. `(container)$ nc -vv <10.0.2.2|some_hostname.net.pod> 12345`

**Describe the results you received:**

Connection refused

**Describe the results you expected:**

Connection accepted

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
sh-5.0$ podman version
Version:      2.0.4
API Version:  1
Go Version:   go1.14.6
Built:        Thu Jan  1 10:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

comments.body:
Having written that and read #6863 more deeply throughout, I'm fairly convinced that the right thing to be considered here is the introduction of a magic DNS record for the host rather than messing with whatever slirp is doing to make 10.0.2.2 work how I mentioned above. What I'm not familiar enough with is how DNS works in podman and if we already have some system which could do this.

EDIT: So from what I can tell, it seems like rootless doesn't get a magic DNS server since it doesn't do any netns/CNI stuff. It seems like this might not be workable and I'll just have to wait for #6863 to be worked through to do this :(

DNS could be complicated, but I could do an entry in /etc/hosts trivially

On Mon, Aug 24, 2020, 19:44 maybe-sybr <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> Having written that and read #6863
> <https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/6863> more deeply
> throughout, I'm fairly convinced that the right thing to be considered here
> is the introduction of a magic DNS record for the host rather than messing
> with whatever slirp is doing to make 10.0.2.2 work how I mentioned above.
> What I'm not familiar enough with is how DNS works in podman and if we
> already have some system which could do this.
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you are subscribed to this thread.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/7432#issuecomment-679420773>,
> or unsubscribe
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AB3AOCEYK7FKCHRXXPUA5WTSCL3OJANCNFSM4QKA3QRA>
> .
>

That's a great idea, @mheon. For me, not having it deal with runtime changes to host addresses/etc would be perfectly acceptable. Not sure how to best pick which IP to inject into the container for general cases though but I'm already using `hostname -I | cut -d ' ' -f1` elsewhere in my dev scripts which works fine for `::` and `0.0` listeners and might be good enough.

Another alternative/follow up might be to support hostAliases in podman play, which I could then populate manually using a `sed` prior to `podman play kube` to act as a poor man's helm. That would shift the onus for picking a sensible IP back to the user (which does invalidate the naive part of this issue and make it play kube specific) and presumably the plumbing would be mostly around whatever `--add-host` does for `podman create` and the kube parsing/generation.

@rhatdan I think the problem I was having was that things like `--add-host` aren't available via the `podman play kube` CLI. I would have definitely used that and been happy if it jammed the host entry into all containers. This is what happens when I try to do something naive like that:

```
$ podman play kube --add-host some-host.local:1.2.3.4 busybox.yaml
Error: unknown flag: --add-host
$ podman --version
podman version 2.0.6
```

Unless something got added recently which I've missed, I don't think there's a way to do what I was asking for.

*However!* Thinking back on the waffle I've commented above, it seems like a super bad idea to be supporting magic like exposing the host to pods/containers by default. If they aren't given a routable IP for the host, they shouldn't have one (even if it would be possible for them to discover it through evil means).

Having an `--add-host` arg passthrough from `play kube` to the eventual `pod create`s it does seems like a sensible solution.

For the moment, I've been doing some janky stuff with an env-var and deprecating that dev environment with usernetes since being able to use k8s services avoids this issue for me entirely and is more representative of prod. So I'm not blocked or needing this particularly strongly.

you could edit the busybox.yaml add this:
```
spec:
  hostAliases:
  - hostnames:
    - test.test.com
    ip: 127.0.0.1
  - hostnames:
    - test1.test.com
    ip: 127.0.0.1
```
and use the `v2.1.0-rc`
Even better, @zhangguanzhang - I just had a look at the diff in #7496 and it sounds like the right solution for what I wanted in this issue. I'll close this with thanks to you and the podman crew!
